# Identifying target processes for microbial electrosynthesis by elementary mode analysis

[*BMC Bioinformatics*](/)
**volume 15**, Article number: 410 (2014)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

6908 Accesses

89 Citations

1 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2/metrics)

## Abstract

### Background

Microbial electrosynthesis and electro fermentation are techniques that aim to optimize microbial production of chemicals and fuels by regulating the cellular redox balance via interaction with electrodes. While the concept is known for decades major knowledge gaps remain, which make it hard to evaluate its biotechnological potential. Here we present an *in silico* approach to identify beneficial production processes for electro fermentation by elementary mode analysis. Since the fundamentals of electron transport between electrodes and microbes have not been fully uncovered yet, we propose different options and discuss their impact on biomass and product yields.

### Results

For the first time 20 different valuable products were screened for their potential to show increased yields during anaerobic electrically enhanced fermentation. Surprisingly we found that an increase in product formation by electrical enhancement is not necessarily dependent on the degree of reduction of the product but rather the metabolic pathway it is derived from. We present a variety of beneficial processes with product yield increases of maximal 36% in reductive and 84% in oxidative fermentations and final theoretical product yields up to 100%. This includes compounds that are already produced at industrial scale such as succinic acid, lysine and diaminopentane as well as potential novel bio-commodities such as isoprene, *para*-hydroxybenzoic acid and *para*-aminobenzoic acid. Furthermore, it is shown that the way of electron transport has major impact on achievable biomass and product yields. The coupling of electron transport to energy conservation could be identified as crucial for most processes.

### Conclusions

This study introduces a powerful tool to determine beneficial substrate and product combinations for electro-fermentation. It also highlights that the maximal yield achievable by bio electrochemical techniques depends strongly on the actual electron transport mechanisms. Therefore it is of great importance to reveal the involved fundamental processes to be able to optimize and advance electro fermentations beyond the level of lab-scale studies.

## Background

Metabolic redox limitations can be a crucial factor determining the viability of an industrial biotechnology process [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR1 "Vickers CE, Klein-Marcuschamer D, Krömer JO: Examining the feasibility of bulk commodity production in Escherichia coli . Biotechnol Lett. 2012, 34 (4): 585-596. 10.1007/s10529-011-0821-3.")]. It could be shown, that increasing the amount of redox cofactors such as NADH or NADPH available to the microorganisms is an effective way to increase the product yield of reduced products such as propane [[2](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR2 "Berrı́os-Rivera SJ, Bennett GN, San K-Y: Metabolic engineering of Escherichia coli: increase of NADH availability by overexpressing an NAD+-dependent formate dehydrogenase. Metab Eng. 2002, 4 (3): 217-229. 10.1006/mben.2002.0227.")],[[3](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR3 "Fasan R, Crook NC, Peters MW, Meinhold P, Buelter T, Landwehr M, Cirino PC, Arnold FH: Improved product-per-glucose yields in P450-dependent propane biotransformations using engineered Escherichia coli . Biotechnol Bioeng. 2011, 108 (3): 500-510. 10.1002/bit.22984.")] and also of commonly produced feed amino acids, e.g. lysine [[4](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR4 "Becker J, Zelder O, Häfner S, Schröder H, Wittmann C: From zero to hero—design-based systems metabolic engineering of Corynebacterium glutamicum for l-lysine production. Metab Eng. 2011, 13 (2): 159-168. 10.1016/j.ymben.2011.01.003.")]. One novel and very promising approach to optimize the cellular redox state for production is to stimulate the metabolism electrically and therefore direct electron flow to desired products. The technique, termed microbial electrosynthesis or electro fermentation, shows potential to increase the efficiency of microbial production by providing additional electron donors or acceptors to the cells [[5](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR5 "Lovley DR: Powering microbes with electricity: direct electron transfer from electrodes to microbes. Environ Microbiol Rep. 2011, 3 (1): 27-35. 10.1111/j.1758-2229.2010.00211.x.")],[[6](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR6 "Rabaey K, Rozendal RA: Microbial electrosynthesis — revisiting the electrical route for microbial production. Nat Rev Micro. 2010, 8 (10): 706-716. 10.1038/nrmicro2422.")]. Even though nowadays already discussed as revolutionising future technology, little is known about its true potential as the fundamental processes still remain unclear [[7](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR7 "Rosenbaum MA, Franks AE: Microbial catalysis in bioelectrochemical technologies: status quo, challenges and perspectives. Appl Microbiol Biotechnol. 2014, 98 (2): 509-518. 10.1007/s00253-013-5396-6.")],[[8](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR8 "Venkata Mohan S, Velvizhi G, Vamshi Krishna K, Lenin Babu M: Microbial catalyzed electrochemical systems: a bio-factory with multi-facet applications. Bioresour Technol. 2014, 165: 355-364. 10.1016/j.biortech.2014.03.048.")].

Before general process design steps can be approached, a better understanding of the overall net benefits of possible target processes is needed [[7](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR7 "Rosenbaum MA, Franks AE: Microbial catalysis in bioelectrochemical technologies: status quo, challenges and perspectives. Appl Microbiol Biotechnol. 2014, 98 (2): 509-518. 10.1007/s00253-013-5396-6.")],[[9](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR9 "Hallenbeck PC: The Future of Biofuels, Biofuels of the Future. Microbial Technologies in Advanced Biofuels Production. 2012, Springer, New York, 261-268. 10.1007/978-1-4614-1208-3_14.")]. These need to feature the production of a higher value carbon-body from a ubiquitous available cheap source by the investment of a reasonable amount of electric energy. While the first published electrically enhanced fermentations display in general proof-of-concept-studies, interesting substrate and product combinations still have to be investigated. The biggest challenge to drive microbial electrochemical technologies beyond fundamental studies is the optimisation of the microbial catalyst. Therefore the actual metabolic processes of microbe and electrode interaction need to be unveiled as they will not only decide about the choice of organism but might also play an important role for the achievable process benefit.

The research field of microbial fuel cells studies microbe-electrode interactions for many years. While key reactions could be identified a thorough understanding of the metabolic response to electrical enhancement has not been achieved yet [[10](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR10 "Patil SA, Haegerhaell C, Gortin L: Electron transfer mechanisms between microorganisms and electrodes in bioelectrochemical systems. Bioanal Rev. 2012, 4: 159-192. 10.1007/s12566-012-0033-x.")],[[11](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR11 "Rosenbaum M, Aulenta F, Villano M, Angenent LT: Cathodes as electron donors for microbial metabolism: which extracellular electron transfer mechanisms are involved?. Bioresour Technol. 2011, 102 (1): 324-333. 10.1016/j.biortech.2010.07.008.")]. The focus of microbial electrosynthesis especially requires more knowledge about cathodic electron transport and extracellular electron transfer (EET) capabilities of model organisms for production such as *E. coli*.

For anodic EET of electrogens such as *Geobacter* and *Shewanella* two main mechanisms are identified: direct electron transfer, which is performed by direct contact between the electrode surface and cellular components of the outer membrane (usually cytochromes), and indirect EET, which includes all forms of electron transfer between electrode and organisms mediated by soluble electron carrier molecules [[12](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR12 "Rabaey K, Boon N, Siciliano SD, Verhaege M, Verstraete W: Biofuel cells select for microbial consortia that self-mediate electron transfer. Appl Environ Microbiol. 2004, 70 (9): 5373-5382. 10.1128/AEM.70.9.5373-5382.2004.")],[[13](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR13 "Thrash JC, Coates JD: Review: direct and indirect electrical stimulation of microbial metabolism. Environ Sci Technol. 2008, 42 (11): 3921-3931. 10.1021/es702668w.")]. First studies on the cathode confirmed the possibility of donating additional electrons to the microbial metabolism by both EET mechanisms and its potential to increase production [[5](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR5 "Lovley DR: Powering microbes with electricity: direct electron transfer from electrodes to microbes. Environ Microbiol Rep. 2011, 3 (1): 27-35. 10.1111/j.1758-2229.2010.00211.x.")],[[14](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR14 "Emde R, Schink B: Enhanced propionate formation by Propionibacterium freudenreichii subsp. freudenreichii in a three-electrode amperometric culture system. Appl Environ Microbiol. 1990, 56 (9): 2771-2776.")]-[[17](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR17 "Nevin KP, Woodard TL, Franks AE, Summers ZM, Lovley DR: Microbial electrosynthesis: feeding microbes electricity to convert carbon dioxide and water to multicarbon extracellular organic compounds. mBio. 2010, 1 (2): e00103-10. 10.1128/mBio.00103-10.")]. But it was also found that the involved mechanisms for electron uptake differ significantly from the known electron donating mechanisms [[10](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR10 "Patil SA, Haegerhaell C, Gortin L: Electron transfer mechanisms between microorganisms and electrodes in bioelectrochemical systems. Bioanal Rev. 2012, 4: 159-192. 10.1007/s12566-012-0033-x.")],[[18](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR18 "Dumas C, Basseguy R, Bergel A: Microbial electrocatalysis with Geobacter sulfurreducens biofilm on stainless steel cathodes. Electrochim acta. 2008, 53 (5): 2494-2500. 10.1016/j.electacta.2007.10.018.")],[[19](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR19 "Strycharz SM, Glaven RH, Coppi MV, Gannon SM, Perpetua LA, Liu A, Nevin KP, Lovley DR: Gene expression and deletion analysis of mechanisms for electron transfer from electrodes to Geobacter sulfurreducens . Bioelectrochemistry. 2011, 80 (2): 142-150. 10.1016/j.bioelechem.2010.07.005.")].

Regardless of the major carbon metabolic pathway the effect of electrical enhancement is typically assumed to result in an increase or decrease of intracellular redox factors such as NADH or NADPH [[8](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR8 "Venkata Mohan S, Velvizhi G, Vamshi Krishna K, Lenin Babu M: Microbial catalyzed electrochemical systems: a bio-factory with multi-facet applications. Bioresour Technol. 2014, 165: 355-364. 10.1016/j.biortech.2014.03.048.")],[[11](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR11 "Rosenbaum M, Aulenta F, Villano M, Angenent LT: Cathodes as electron donors for microbial metabolism: which extracellular electron transfer mechanisms are involved?. Bioresour Technol. 2011, 102 (1): 324-333. 10.1016/j.biortech.2010.07.008.")]. Electron transfer towards an anode is assumed to be coupled to energy conservation where the electrode functions as solid final acceptor during respiration [[20](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR20 "Torres CI, Marcus AK, Lee HS, Parameswaran P, Krajmalnik‐Brown R, Rittmann BE: A kinetic perspective on extracellular electron transfer by anode‐respiring bacteria. FEMS Microbiol Rev. 2010, 34 (1): 3-17. 10.1111/j.1574-6976.2009.00191.x.")],[[21](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR21 "Rittmann BE, Torres CI, Marcus AK: Understanding the Distinguishing Features of a Microbial Fuel Cell as a Biomass-Based Renewable Energy Technology. Emerging Environmental Technologies. 2008, Springer, Netherlands, 1-28. 10.1007/978-1-4020-8786-8_1.")]. However the exact ratio of electrons and protons that are transported remains purely speculative. Furthermore, it is not known by which mechanisms non-metal-respiring organisms might transfer electrons to an anode and whether that transfer promotes ATP generation or not [[22](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR22 "Logan BE: Exoelectrogenic bacteria that power microbial fuel cells. Nat Rev Microbiol. 2009, 7 (5): 375-381. 10.1038/nrmicro2113.")]. Even though there is even less information available about cathodic electron transfer there is a general concept proposed that assumes the creation of a proton motive force by intracellular electron consumption, which is available for ATP synthesis [[5](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR5 "Lovley DR: Powering microbes with electricity: direct electron transfer from electrodes to microbes. Environ Microbiol Rep. 2011, 3 (1): 27-35. 10.1111/j.1758-2229.2010.00211.x.")],[[11](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR11 "Rosenbaum M, Aulenta F, Villano M, Angenent LT: Cathodes as electron donors for microbial metabolism: which extracellular electron transfer mechanisms are involved?. Bioresour Technol. 2011, 102 (1): 324-333. 10.1016/j.biortech.2010.07.008.")],[[23](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR23 "Nevin KP, Hensley SA, Franks AE, Summers ZM, Ou J, Woodard TL, Snoeyenbos-West OL, Lovley DR: Electrosynthesis of organic compounds from carbon dioxide is catalyzed by a diversity of acetogenic microorganisms. Appl Environ Microbiol. 2011, 77 (9): 2882-2886. 10.1128/AEM.02642-10.")],[[24](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR24 "Pandit AV, Mahadevan R: In silico characterization of microbial electrosynthesis for metabolic engineering of biochemicals. Microb Cell Fact. 2011, 10 (1): 76-76. 10.1186/1475-2859-10-76.")]. In mediated electrically enhanced fermentations of *Actinobacillus succinogenes* Park and Zeikus observed an electron flow from the cathode into the product succinate [[25](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR25 "Park DH, Laivenieks M, Guettler MV, Jain MK, Zeikus JG: Microbial utilization of electrically reduced neutral Red as the sole electron donor for growth and metabolite production. Appl Environ Microbiol. 1999, 65 (7): 2912-2917.")]. Simultaneously, the electron transfer via the reduced mediator Neutral Red and the proton-pumping fumarate reductase complex of *A. succinogenes* induced proton translocation and therefore increased ATP synthesis [[26](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR26 "Park DH, Zeikus JG: Utilization of electrically reduced neutral Red byActinobacillus succinogenes: physiological function of neutral Red in membrane-driven fumarate reduction and energy conservation. J Bacteriol. 1999, 181 (8): 2403-2410.")]. While the activity of the proton pumping fumarate reductase of *A. succinogenes* is most likely solely responsible for the reported increase in proton flux through the ATPase complex, nowadays the theory about cathodic EET generally assumes that all electrons supplied by EET enter the cytoplasm as negative charge and catalyse intracellular, proton consuming reductions. Simultaneously, the proton consumption would lead to a proton gradient across the inner membrane that drives ATP synthesis [[5](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR5 "Lovley DR: Powering microbes with electricity: direct electron transfer from electrodes to microbes. Environ Microbiol Rep. 2011, 3 (1): 27-35. 10.1111/j.1758-2229.2010.00211.x.")]. But is this the only possibility? Observed is poor growth in very thin biofilms on cathodes [[15](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR15 "Gregory KB, Bond DR, Lovley DR: Graphite electrodes as electron donors for anaerobic respiration. Environ Microbiol. 2004, 6 (6): 596-604. 10.1111/j.1462-2920.2004.00593.x.")],[[18](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR18 "Dumas C, Basseguy R, Bergel A: Microbial electrocatalysis with Geobacter sulfurreducens biofilm on stainless steel cathodes. Electrochim acta. 2008, 53 (5): 2494-2500. 10.1016/j.electacta.2007.10.018.")], which seems to be surprising if cathodic EET could deliver redox power as well as energy (NADH and ATP). So the questions are: Are the protons involved in cathodic electron transfer generally available for ATP generation? And does electron transfer towards an anode always occur by the respiratory chain which thereby creates a proton gradient? What other ways of EET could occur and how would this impact production?

Aim of this work is to present a useful analysis tool, which is able to identify beneficial production processes for microbial electrosynthesis, and at the same time enables insight into the energy conservation possibilities during anaerobic electrically enhanced fermentation. Using *in silico* approach to calculate the metabolic impact of different electron transport routes during electrically enhanced fermentation enables the evaluation of different mechanisms while current knowledge gaps remain. Pandit et al. recently presented a first computational approach that characterized the general role of bio electrosynthesis in chemical production using a genome scale metabolic model of *E. coli* [[24](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR24 "Pandit AV, Mahadevan R: In silico characterization of microbial electrosynthesis for metabolic engineering of biochemicals. Microb Cell Fact. 2011, 10 (1): 76-76. 10.1186/1475-2859-10-76.")]. Within their model it is assumed that cathodic electrons enter the metabolism and directly reduce NAD+ to NADH. Analogous to the theory discussed before the authors precariously assume the creation of a proton motive force that drives ATP synthesis even though the fumarate reductase of *E. coli* is, unlike the one of *A. succinogenes,* a non-proton-pumping enzyme [[27](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR27 "Iverson TM, Luna-Chavez C, Cecchini G, Rees DC: Structure of the Escherichia coli fumarate reductase respiratory complex. Science. 1999, 284 (5422): 1961-1966. 10.1126/science.284.5422.1961.")]. Not surprisingly they report an increase of ATP yield caused by electron supply. We regard extracellular electron transport coupled and uncoupled to ATP synthesis and discuss the properties of both options to boost the production of various valuable products. Four different electron transport scenarios for mediated cathodic and anodic EET are described in the following paragraph and are visualised in Figure [1](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#Fig1)A and B.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs12859-014-0410-2/MediaObjects/12859_2014_Article_410_Fig1_HTML.jpg)

**Models for extracellular electron transport mechanisms coupled and uncoupled to energy conservation. (A)** Schematic image of two different electron transport mechanisms between cathodes and the microbial metabolism analysed within this study. *Cat1*) Electron transport via a mediator and a cascade of membrane bound complexes (e.g. cytochromes) with simultaneous ATP generation; *Cat2*) Direct reduction of NAD to NADH by electrons and protons by membrane bound enzymes (e.g. hydrogenases) or diffusion of the mediator molecule. **(B)** Two different models for microbial interaction with an anode as electron sink. *An1*) Electrons from the quinone pool are transferred to the electron mediator by membrane-bound enzymes such as NADH-Ubiquinone oxidoreductase. As these complexes are proton pumping the created gradient can be used for ATP generation. *An2*) Electrons and protons are transferred simultaneously without creating a membrane potential.

### Cathode 1 (*Cat1*)

Mediator oxidation occurs on outer membrane cytochromes that transfer the electrons into the organism and finally onto NAD. Charge-imbalance creates driving force for 1 proton per electron to enter the cytosol and drive ATP-synthase (transport of 3 protons catalyses the generation of one molecule ATP).

### Cathode 2 (*Cat2*)

The mediator transfers its electrons and protons directly onto NAD without creating a driving force for ATP synthesis. This could happen by diffusion into the cytoplasm or catalyzed by enzymes such as hydrogenases.

### Anode 1 (*An1*)

Electrons from the Quinone-pool are transferred to the mediator molecule by membrane-bound cytochromes of the respiratory chain. Running the electron transport chain via Quinones creates a proton gradient that drives ATP synthesis.

### Anode 2 (*An2*)

The anode acts like an electron sink by directly accepting electrons from NADH. This could happen either catalyzed by membrane-bound enzymes such as hydrogenases or by diffusion of the mediator into the cytoplasm.

Note that apart from the mediator diffusion model all models could theoretically also happen as direct electron transfer between the electrode surface and the cellular membrane.

### Metabolic modelling by elementary mode analysis

We created core networks of metabolic carbon pathways to determine the effect of electrical enhancement through the different EET ways on production. The tool chosen for the metabolic analysis is elementary mode analysis (EMA), which determines all possible solutions of the metabolic matrix by calculating a unique set of so called *elementary flux modes* (*efm*s) [[28](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR28 "Schuster S, Dandekar T, Fell DA: Detection of elementary flux modes in biochemical networks: a promising tool for pathway analysis and metabolic engineering. Trends Biotechnol. 1999, 17 (2): 53-60. 10.1016/S0167-7799(98)01290-6.")]. Each *elementary flux mode* pictures the proposed cellular metabolism in steady-state conditions and together all *efm*s span the complete solution space for each network. Within this solution space we can determine maximum yields for certain products and reconstruct carbon fluxes within the network for example to study changes in by-product formation. The advantage of EMA over other modelling approaches is the calculation of ALL solutions rather than only one best solution (e.g. in flux balance analysis). Thereby we can not only assess theoretical maximum yields for production and biomass formation but are also able to compare all possible metabolic flux distributions, which presents a more holistic view of the impact of each EET model [[29](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR29 "Trinh CT, Wlaschin A, Srienc F: Elementary mode analysis: a useful metabolic pathway analysis tool for characterizing cellular metabolism. Appl Microbiol Biotechnol. 2009, 81 (5): 813-826. 10.1007/s00253-008-1770-1.")].

Elementary mode analysis is based purely on stoichiometry of the reaction equations and steady-state conditions of the organism. Therefore the solution space can be regarded as outer boundaries of the metabolic possibilities. Here we use this effectively to determine the maximum theoretical possible advantage of EET on production. Actual *in vivo* yields will lie inside the determined solutions space. However they will usually be lower than the theoretical maximum yield and will depend on many factors such as thermodynamics, enzyme kinetics, gene regulation and product toxicity, which are not taken into account here.

The presented metabolic analysis was implemented exemplarily for the central carbon metabolism of *Escherichia coli* as model organism for industrial biotechnology. Tools for its genetic modification are well established which makes it an attractive host for the production of various compounds. Even though *E. coli* does not show a comparable electrical activity to *Geobacter* or *Shewanella* species, it was shown to be able to exchange electrons with electrodes via soluble mediator molecules [[30](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR30 "McKinlay JB, Zeikus JG: Extracellular iron reduction is mediated in part by neutral red and hydrogenase in Escherichia coli . Appl Environ Microbiol. 2004, 70 (6): 3467-3474. 10.1128/AEM.70.6.3467-3474.2004.")]-[[32](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR32 "Xie X-H, Li EL, Tang ZK: Sudden emergence of redox active Escherichia coli phenotype: cyclic voltammetric evidence of the overlapping pathways. Int J Electrochem Sci. 2010, 5: 1070-1081.")]. Furthermore recent studies report successful transfer of functional molecules from the electron transport chain of *Shewanella oneidensis* into *E. coli* and therefore suggest that the microbe could be modified for optimized electron exchange mechanisms [[33](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR33 "Jensen HM, Albers AE, Malley KR, Londer YY, Cohen BE, Helms BA, Weigele P, Groves JT, Ajo-Franklin CM: Engineering of a synthetic electron conduit in living cells. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A. 2010, 107 (45): 19213-19218. 10.1073/pnas.1009645107.")],[[34](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR34 "TerAvest MA, Zajdel TJ, Ajo‐Franklin CM: The Mtr pathway of shewanella oneidensis MR‐1 couples substrate utilization to current production in escherichia coli. Chem Electro Chem. 2014, 1 (11): 1874-1879.")].

## Results and discussion

In the following sections we present calculated carbon yields for the production of biomass and various valuable compounds including carboxylic acids, alcohols and aromatics via electrically enhanced fermentation. The initial idea of microbial electrosynthesis was to start from the fully oxidized substrate CO2 and provide all electrons by an electrode. But a disadvantage of using CO2 as sole substrate is the extremely high electron demand and energy limitation by the strictly anaerobic pathways such as the Wood Ljungdahl pathway. Hence many approaches regard the conversion of organic molecules from waste streams, such as acetate, lactate or glycerol by non-acetogenic organisms as more beneficial [[35](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR35 "Desloover J, Arends J, Hennebel T, Rabaey K: Operational and technical considerations for microbial electrosynthesis. Biochem Soc Trans. 2012, 40 (6): 1233-1238. 10.1042/BST20120111.")],[[36](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR36 "Rabaey K, Girguis P, Nielsen LK: Metabolic and practical considerations on microbial electrosynthesis. Curr Opin Biotechnol. 2011, 22 (3): 371-377. 10.1016/j.copbio.2011.01.010.")]. Within this work we focus on microbial electrosynthesis from substrates other than CO2, mainly glucose and glycerol, a process which is often referred to as “electro fermentation” [[7](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR7 "Rosenbaum MA, Franks AE: Microbial catalysis in bioelectrochemical technologies: status quo, challenges and perspectives. Appl Microbiol Biotechnol. 2014, 98 (2): 509-518. 10.1007/s00253-013-5396-6.")],[[37](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR37 "Angenent LT, Rosenbaum MA: Microbial electrocatalysis to guide biofuel and biochemical bioprocessing. Biofuels. 2013, 4 (2): 131-134. 10.4155/bfs.12.93.")]. Sugar fermentations are dominating in bio-industry and were therefore investigated to determine the potential of electrical enhancement to boost these processes [[38](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR38 "Jang Y-S, Kim B, Shin JH, Choi YJ, Choi S, Song CW, Lee J, Park HG, Lee SY: Bio-based production of C2–C6 platform chemicals. Biotechnol Bioeng. 2012, 109 (10): 2437-2459. 10.1002/bit.24599.")]. As a second substrate of interest glycerol was chosen as it represents a cheap C-source often produced as a waste in biodiesel production [[39](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR39 "Clomburg JM, Gonzalez R: Anaerobic fermentation of glycerol: a platform for renewable fuels and chemicals. Trends Biotechnol. 2013, 31 (1): 20-28. 10.1016/j.tibtech.2012.10.006.")]. Its more reductive state compared to glucose suggests it could result in higher yields when converted into more reduced compounds and require less additional electrons [[40](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR40 "Gonzalez R, Murarka A, Dharmadi Y, Yazdani SS: A new model for the anaerobic fermentation of glycerol in enteric bacteria: Trunk and auxiliary pathways in Escherichia coli . Metab Eng. 2008, 10 (5): 234-245. 10.1016/j.ymben.2008.05.001.")]. A current review by Jang et al. summarizes important C2-C6-products and their biological production [[38](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#ref-CR38 "Jang Y-S, Kim B, Shin JH, Choi YJ, Choi S, Song CW, Lee J, Park HG, Lee SY: Bio-based production of C2–C6 platform chemicals. Biotechnol Bioeng. 2012, 109 (10): 2437-2459. 10.1002/bit.24599.")]. We implemented all anaerobic production pathways in our metabolic network and analysed the theoretical yields of each compound under electrical enhancement. Figure [2](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#Fig2) shows the metabolic fluxmap of the presented *E. coli* carbon network including all product pathways. A full list of all maximum product yields, with and without biomass formation as well as the number of computed *elementary flux modes* for each substrate and product combination can be found in Additional file [1](/articles/10.1186/s12859-014-0410-2#MOESM1).

--- SOURCE 3: Recent advances in microbial electrosynthesis system: Metabolic ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1369703X23001237

SUMMARY:
Microbial electrosynthesis (MES) presents a promising technology for bio-electrochemical conversion of released CO 2 and organic wastes into electricity and biofuel-chemicals using external-powered and/or self-powered microbial oxidation/reduction processes. The previously published review articles mainly summarized the concept of MES, biocathodic production processes, variety of substrates used, the architecture of electrode materials and bioreactor designs, microbial engineering and electron transfer pathways, potential synthesis of value-added products, state-of-art of the MES, and critical review on the recent development of the MES technology (Table 1). Therefore, to achieve the full potential and successful commercial application of MES, a deep understanding of microbial metabolic investigations, bioengineering, electron transfer mechanisms and process optimizing parameters is required to establish MES technology for CO 2 sequestration and value-added products/chemical synthesis.

Recent advances in microbial electrosynthesis system: Metabolic investigation and process optimization
======================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Abdullah Al-Mamun a, Waqar Ahmed a b, Tahereh Jafary a c, Jagdeep Kumar Nayak a, Ali Al-Nuaimi a, Ahmad Sana a

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bej.2023.108928](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bej.2023.108928 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S1369703X23001237&orderBeanReset=true)

Highlights
----------

*   •Recent advances in microbial electrosynthesis system for metabolic investigation. 
*   •Summarized and analyzed the process optimization parameters in MES. 
*   •CO 2 sequestration and value-added products synthesis by bioelectricity. 
*   •Diversified application of electrogenic microbes and metabolic pathways. 

Abstract
--------

The intensified burning of fossil fuels and the discharging of industrial wastes are severe threats to the environment. The released CO 2 and organic fractions of industrial and municipal wastes exacerbate global warming. Converting the released CO 2 and organic wastes into beneficial electricity and biofuel-chemicals is deemed an environmental necessity. Microbial electrosynthesis (MES) presents a promising technology for bio-electrochemical conversion of released CO 2 and organic wastes into electricity and biofuel-chemicals using external-powered and/or self-powered microbial oxidation/reduction processes. The MES system consists of anodic and cathodic processes. The technology mostly relies on the capacity of electron transfer from electroactive biofilm to the electrode for reducing organics into value-added chemicals and sustaining their respiration and growth. The current review aims to summarize and explore the diversified application of electrogenic microbes and their metabolic pathways of electron transfer. It also summarizes the MES reactor design and operational parameters that influence the catalysis of biofilm and hence, the system performance. The review concludes with a critical evaluation of technical challenges that should be overcome before large-scale implementation. Furthermore, various recommendations on technical perspectives for successful implementation and application, including future research directions, are presented in this study.

Introduction
------------

The synthesis of bio-fuels and value-added chemicals from biomass feedstocks (e.g., sludge, sediments), organic wastes (e.g., lignocellulose, food processing and refinery wastes) and livestock manure (e.g., cattle, poultry farms) has been commenced worldwide at both laboratory and pilot scales [1], [2]. These organic wastes are believed as an important alternative to fossil fuel derivatives [3], [4]. The organic fractions could be converted into energy and bio-fuel chemicals through a sustainable and eco-friendly bio-electrochemical system (BES) approach [5], [6]. Generally, the BES is comprised of an anode and cathode chambers separated by an ion-exchange membrane (IEM). However, some BES reactors were also studied without separators [7]. The BES utilizes electro-active (EA) biofilms as catalysts to drive electrochemical reactions [8], [9], [10], [11]. Such electrochemical conversions consist of oxidation and reduction reactions at anode and cathode, respectively, where the electrodes with the biofilm catalysts are connected to an external circuit [12], [13], [14], [15]. Depending on the operational configuration, BESs comprised of microbial fuel cell (MFC) [11], [16], [17], microbial desalination cell (MDC) [18], [19], [20], [21], [22], [23], microbial electrolysis cell (MEC) [23], [24] and microbial electrosynthesis (MES) [25], [26]. MFC can harness electrical energy through the oxidation of organic substrates by anodic EA biofilms [5], [27]. Similar to MDC, it can produce electricity as well as achieve remarkable desalination of untreated seawater [22], [28]. In the case of MEC configuration, EA biofilm degraded organics and inorganics using external electrical input to produce bio-hydrogen [29], [30], [31]. These reactors have been extensively studied for various renewable energy/water treatment applications during the last decade. However, all the above BESs face technological and economic limitations for producing bio-energy and bio-hydrogen [32], [33], [34].

MES is a promising technology for energy production through the oxidation of organics on the anode and recovering of value-added chemicals by reducing CO 2 and organics on the cathode. Both the anodic and the cathodic conversions are catalyzed by EA biofilms [32], [33]. Fig. 1 shows possible bio-conversion routes in MES for concurrent sequestration of CO 2, recovery of electrical energy, and value-added chemicals. An important aspect of MES research is to investigate thoroughly the different types of microbes and their mechanisms of accepting the electrons from the electrode to reduce terminal electron acceptors [38]. In the early stages of MES development, mediators improved metal oxidation and the glutamic acid yield from glucose fermentation [35], [36]. In addition, the mediators assisted the methanogenesis in MES reactors that were inoculated by either mixed-culture or pure-culture of _Escherichia coli_, _Klebsiella pneumonia_, and _Zymomonas mobilis_ species [37], [38]. However, it was later discovered that a biofilm of _Geobacter_ species on the cathode could reduce fumarate to succinate, thereby affirming the mediator-free direct electron transfer [43]. Therefore, the investigation of electron transfer mechanisms and the efficient utilization of the revealed electrons in the synthesis of value-added products could improve the performance of MES. In addition, the utilization of mixed-biofilms with diversified metabolism, redox mediators and external e- supply could improve MES performances [32].

It has been reported that various kinds of biodegradable organic-containing streams, especially the discharges from food, beverage, agriculture and refinery industries, were used as electron donors in MES to produce value-added chemicals on the cathode [44], [45], [46], [47]. Studies related to CO 2 utilization as cathodic substrates and its conversion to methane, acetate and other biofuel chemicals have also been extensively reported in the literature [33], [44], [45], [46]. Such conversion of CO 2 into value-added chemicals was considered a promising approach for carbon capture and utilization technology, an important measure of mitigating greenhouse gas emissions [51], [52]. The utilization of CO 2 as substrates in any industrial process might be considered a great blessing for the eco-friendly development of human civilization on earth. Such sequestration of CO 2 was technically feasible due to its abundance in the atmosphere, mainly due to anthropogenic emissions from industrial, agricultural and transportation activities [53]. However, the conversion of CO 2 added several technical disadvantages to the MES system, including (1) significant consumption of electrons for the synthesis of organics and (2) an external supply of energy to activate the autotrophic growth of biofilms [32]. In addition, the imbalance growth of biofilms community at both the cathode and the anode slows the reduction process of CO 2 [49], [50]. To resolve these issues, extensive research is needed to explore the economic, electrochemical and environmental feasibility of MES.

A variety of microorganisms can be used to reduce substrates on the cathode of the MES using CO 2 as the source of carbon. For example, _Chemolihoautotrophic_ microorganisms can be used to reduce inorganic substrates on the cathode to harness their metabolic energy [56]. _Photoautotrophs_ also utilize radiant energy (light energy) for their metabolic activities. Microorganisms, which directly accept electrons from the cathode to reduce substrates, are termed electrotrophs [57]. Studies [35], [58] show that acetogenic bacteria (_Sporomusa spp. and Clostridium spp._) are employed to produce acetate and ethanol by utilizing the gaseous substrates (CO 2, N 2, CO and H 2) on the cathode. The autotrophic bacteria capable of receiving an electron directly from the electrode were also utilized as bio-catalysts during the earlier stage of MES development [55]. It has been found that methane and acetate are the commonly synthesized products during MES operations when using methanogenic and acetogenic cultures, respectively [54], [55]. Forming these desired products requires the selection of suitable microorganisms, which influences the quality and quantity of the end products [48]. During the earlier stage of bio-electrochemical development, pure cultures are utilized for the production of bio-commodities. However, the use of mixed cultures in recent years increased the yield and process performance [58], [59], [60], [61].

The selection of electrode materials impacts the yield and the rate of bio-conversion of chemicals in MES [58], [61]. An ideal electrode material capable of maximizing the performance of MES should have a higher surface area for biofilm development, ease of attachment of biofilms, and high conductivity of biofilm to transfer electrons. An electrode modification by either inserting proper positive charge or fixing the redox molecules on the surface has improved the biofilm-electrode affinity and interaction [62], [63], [64], [65]. The rate of electron transfer between the microorganisms and the electrode surface is normally controlled by reducing the amount of activation energy. The reduction in activation energy was achieved by the application of metal catalysts on the electrode surface [66], [67], [68], [69], [70]. So far, the pathways of microbial electron transfer have been identified by either cytochrome C or nano-pilli or extra-cellular enzyme. However, not all microbes could transfer electrons by all the above three identified pathways. These pathways are very specific for particular microorganisms applied in the MES [40], [70]. Moreover, the electrode with a rough and porous surface could be favorable for developing the electroactive biofilm layers and distributing the substrates [72], [73], [74], [75], [76].

Despite the extensive studies on multiple aspects, the MES technology faces different challenges, i.e., the disproportionate growth rate of anodic and cathodic microbes, cell overpotentials, and energy losses. The previously published review articles mainly summarized the concept of MES, biocathodic production processes, variety of substrates used, the architecture of electrode materials and bioreactor designs, microbial engineering and electron transfer pathways, potential synthesis of value-added products, state-of-art of the MES, and critical review on the recent development of the MES technology (Table 1). However, none of the review articles summarized the parameters for process optimization and types and diversity of microorganisms for effective metabolic investigation. Therefore, to achieve the full potential and successful commercial application of MES, a deep understanding of microbial metabolic investigations, bioengineering, electron transfer mechanisms and process optimizing parameters is required to establish MES technology for CO 2 sequestration and value-added products/chemical synthesis. Therefore, this review article provides recent and comprehensive knowledge on microorganism species, electron transfer routes and operational challenges associated with MES operation.

Environmental perspective of MES
--------------------------------

MES not only synthesizes biochemicals, but also treats different categories of waste-streams and pollutants, such as discharged streams from fermentation, diluted glycerol and other organic wastes from biodiesel plants. All kinds of produced organic effluents may be converted to value-added chemicals by integrating MES technology [45]. Hence, the integration of MES with the bio-refineries could achieve resource recovery from the waste organics such as oily residues, organic residues, aquatic

Microorganisms involved in the MES process
------------------------------------------

Microorganisms act as biocatalysts in MES reactors to accomplish bio-electrochemical reactions [92]. A variety of microbes for the synthesis of products involving both pure and mixed cultures have been investigated by several studies to evaluate the performance levels of MES [93], [94], [95], [96]. Electron transfer mechanisms in bio-electrochemical process
-----------------------------------------------------------

In BES, great varieties of microbes are used to exchange electrons with solid-state electrodes. Thus, microbe-electrode interactions are deemed as one of the key factors for microbial metabolism, which influences the production of chemicals and fuels [128]. However, the electron transfer rate between microbes and electrodes is one of the fundamental criteria for determining the MES performance. Effective parameters on MES performance
---------------------------------------

Generally, the MES cell comprises an anode chamber and a cathode chamber separated by an ion-selective membrane, electrodes to which biofilms are attached, and an external electrical circuit with/without a source of applied potential. Fig. 1 demonstrates the basic concept of MES, showing the routes of substrate circulations with simultaneous recovery of energy and value-added chemicals. Current challenges and future developments
------------------------------------------

The continuous development of MES technology through successive evolution in the selection of reactor designs, materials of construction, type of organisms, appropriate substrates and optimized operating conditions have led this technology to potential conversions of various types of biomass/waste streams into value-added commodities. The MES can represent a sustainable approach as redox reactions for substrate degradation as well as product synthesis can be powered through renewable resources. 

Conclusions
-----------

MES is a promising bioelectrochemical approach for reducing various waste streams into value-added biofuels and biocommodities. This review article thoroughly explains the different microorganism types and their electron transfer mechanisms. Moreover, the article investigates the influence of various design and operational parameters of MES. enriched_points.expanded_content: Selecting microorganisms involves assessing their electron transfer efficiency, metabolic pathways, substrate utilization, and product formation capabilities, which influence overall performance in microbial electrosynthesis processes.

Cultivation techniques include optimizing growth conditions, such as nutrient availability, pH, temperature, and electrode materials, to enhance biofilm formation and microbial activity for efficient electrosynthesis.
Enhancing microbial performance can be achieved through genetic engineering, optimizing electron transfer mechanisms, and utilizing mixed cultures to improve product yield and process robustness.
